turns.text: Hello Jacqui, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for asking to talk to me. Honestly, it’s a lot right now. I’m only in my first trimester, but it already feels like I’ve been running a marathon every single day. 

I’m sitting at Park Street station, just staring at the map, trying to figure out how many transfers I have to make to get to this specialist appointment. People think because I’m in Boston, I should have it easy, but they don't see the reality of the buses being late or the cost of the CharlieCard adding up. I’m balancing a job, trying to keep my head above water, and then there’s the pre-eclampsia stuff they keep throwing at me. Every time I get in front of a doctor, they just talk about "risks" and "complications." They look at my chart like it’s a math problem to solve, not a person who is doing this all on her own.

I’m scared. I haven't really told anyone that part, because what’s the point? It’s not like they can make the fear of the actual delivery go away. I just feel like I'm holding my breath waiting for something to go wrong. It’s hard to stay calm for the baby when your blood pressure is already tied to your rent money.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly? I guess I thought it would be... well, partnership. In my head, I pictured someone you could actually talk to—not just a face behind a clipboard who's checking a box and looking at the clock because they’re ten minutes behind schedule. I thought they’d ask how I was doing, not just how my blood pressure was doing.

In reality, it’s just cold. It’s all "high risk" this and "monitor" that. It feels like I’m being managed like a piece of equipment that might break, rather than a person growing a life. When I walk into those clinics, I feel so small. I’m just a chart number in a room full of people who clearly have cars and partners holding their hands.

Nobody has ever asked me, "Jacqui, how are you actually going to get here?" or "How are you going to manage the stress of all this by yourself?" It’s just, "Make sure you’re here at 8:00 AM on Tuesday for your scan." I had this naive idea that the healthcare system was a safety net, but lately, it feels more like an obstacle course. I’m exhausted just thinking about the next appointment, and that’s not how it’s supposed to be, right?
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I thought I’d feel strong, you know? Since this is my third, I told myself I’d be a pro at this. I thought I’d be able to just "breeze through" the early stages because I’ve been down this road before. I imagined feeling this sense of steady, quiet confidence. I wanted to be the calm, capable mom who had it all under control.

Instead, I feel like I’m unraveling. It’s like the fear has its own heartbeat now. I’m not calm—I’m terrified. Every time I get a headache or feel a little off, my brain goes straight to the worst-case scenario. It’s this constant, gnawing anxiety that I didn’t expect. I thought I’d have this reservoir of experience to pull from, but it’s actually the opposite. Because I know more about what *can* go wrong, I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop.

It’s just... lonely. Being "ready" feels different when you realize you’re the only one responsible for your own safety and the baby’s. I spend so much of my energy trying to mask the panic so I don't look like a "problem patient." I wanted to feel ready, but mostly I just feel like I'm white-knuckling it through every single day.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I think I expected those apps to be like having a friend in my pocket. I wanted them to be supportive, maybe a little encouraging, and actually helpful with the real-life stuff—like how to navigate working while sick, or where to find help if you’re struggling to make ends meet. 

But when I actually started using them? It felt like they were written for a version of me that doesn't exist. They talk about "prenatal yoga classes" or "healthy organic snacks" or "sharing the journey with your partner." It’s like, *what partner?* And I’m not doing yoga; I’m fighting for a seat on the Red Line so I don't faint on the way to a clinic. 

It’s all so curated and... white-gloved. They give you these checklists of milestones, but they don't give you a checklist for what to do when you’re overwhelmed by the cost of your prescriptions or the weight of being "high risk." Every notification feels like a reminder of what I don’t have. Instead of feeling informed, I just end up feeling behind and totally out of touch with the "ideal" pregnancy experience they’re selling. It’s not information I need; it’s just a reminder of how much harder my reality is than what everyone else is posting about.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is definitely the tone. The apps are all sunshine and rainbows, talking about "bonding" and "nesting" like I’m supposed to be decorating a nursery I can’t afford. They make it sound like a beautiful, magical milestone. But then I get to the doctor, and the tone is purely clinical, cold, and heavy. It’s all "your blood pressure is elevated" or "we need to monitor you closely for pre-eclampsia." 

There’s this massive, empty space in the middle where I actually live. Neither the apps nor the doctors seem to acknowledge the life I’m living in between those two extremes. The apps ignore the medical reality—they never tell you how to talk to your boss about needing time off for constant, urgent appointments—and the doctors completely ignore the human reality. 

My doctor sees a diagnosis; the app sees a generic "mother-to-be." Neither of them sees *me*. I feel like I’m caught in this weird tug-of-war where one side is trying to sell me a fairytale and the other is trying to manage a crisis, and I’m just trying to figure out how to pay for the T pass to get from the "fairytale" to the "crisis." It feels like I'm not even part of the conversation, honestly.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

Well, when I knew I was pregnant—and knowing my history—I guess I expected a plan. Not just a generic pamphlet, but a conversation. I wanted someone to sit down with me and say, "Okay, Jacqui, we know your history, here is how we’re going to manage this together so you don't have to carry the fear alone." I expected them to be the experts who would take the weight off my shoulders because they’d seen this before. I thought there would be a team approach—maybe someone to help with the logistics or at least someone to talk to about the anxiety, since the physical stuff is so tied to my stress levels.

But what actually happened? Nothing like that. When I brought up my concerns, it was like they were just waiting for me to hit a threshold where it became a "real" emergency. It felt like I was being told, "We’ll cross that bridge when we get to it," which is the last thing you want to hear when you’re already scared. I felt like I had to prove I was "high risk enough" to warrant any real attention. There was no preparation, just observation. I left the office feeling more like a ticking time bomb than a person being cared for. It’s like they’re waiting for the crisis to arrive instead of helping me prevent it.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

That’s a hard one to answer. I think, like a lot of women, I had this picture of a "team." I imagined someone who would be there to help with the logistics—the endless appointments, the phone calls to insurance, the late-night worries. I pictured someone who would look at the budget with me, maybe even drive me to the appointments so I wouldn't have to deal with the public transit panic. I thought the emotional weight would be shared, that the "high risk" part wouldn't be something I was carrying entirely on my own. I wanted someone who would actually *care* about the details, not just show up for the big moments.

The reality? He’s not here. There is no team. When I realized I was doing this completely solo, it was like the floor dropped out from under me. It’s not just the absence of a partner; it’s the absence of that safety net that I thought was a given. It makes every single hurdle—the bus fares, the stress at work, the scary, quiet nights—feel ten times heavier. I expected partnership, but I got complete isolation. I’m just trying to make it through, but it’s really hard to feel like I’m "ready" for a baby when I’m still mourning the help I thought I’d have.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Honestly, that burden has been there from the very first positive test. Every single moment of this pregnancy is a calculation I have to make alone. It’s like I’m working a second, unpaid job just to stay "compliant" with my medical care.

It’s the constant math—if I take a half-day for this appointment, does that mean I can’t afford the medication co-pay? If I’m late to work because the bus broke down, will my boss hold it against me? I’m the one who has to memorize the specialists' names, keep track of my blood pressure logs, and figure out the transit routes. The doctors give me instructions like, "Avoid stress," which is almost laughable because I *am* the stress-management system for this pregnancy.

What drives it? It’s the sheer lack of any other option. When you don't have a partner to lean on and you aren't exactly swimming in money, there is no one else to delegate to. The system assumes you have a support person to drive you and a cushion to handle the missed hours at work. Because I don't have those things, the responsibility—every ounce of it—sits right on my chest. I feel like if I miss one detail, the whole thing falls apart. It’s not just a burden; it’s a constant, suffocating pressure to be perfect while everything around me is so unstable.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't actually lost a baby—I've been lucky in that way, and I know how rare that is, especially with all the complications I deal with. But even without that history, the fear of losing this one is just as sharp. It’s like a constant hum in the back of my mind. 

Because I’m already showing signs of pre-eclampsia, I go into every single appointment expecting them to tell me that something is wrong. I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop, and honestly, the system does nothing to settle that fear. They treat me like I’m a problem to be fixed, not a person who is terrified of losing her baby. 

If I *had* lost a baby before, I would have expected them to hold my hand a little more, you know? To maybe acknowledge that the trauma of the past makes the stress of the present so much worse. But the support available to me now is just... clinical. It's sterile. Nobody asks, "Are you scared?" They just ask, "Is your BP still high?" They don't provide any emotional space for the possibility of loss. I’m just out here by myself, navigating this, and I feel like if the worst did happen, I’d be left to deal with the silence all on my own, just like I’m dealing with the appointments.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I guess I imagined something warmer. I pictured a clean, bright office, maybe a nurse who would smile and say, "Congratulations, we’re going to take good care of you." I thought there would be a conversation—maybe a folder with helpful info, a sense of "we're in this together." I really thought the first visit would be about building a relationship with a provider who would see me through the next nine months.

The reality was such a shock. The clinic is crowded, loud, and feels more like a DMV than a place where new life is starting. Everyone is rushed, looking at their monitors, not at me. The air is heavy with that smell of antiseptic and anxiety. Instead of a conversation, it was just a series of commands: "Step on the scale," "Roll up your sleeve," "Wait in that chair." When they finally got to my chart and saw the complications, the mood shifted to something colder. It was like I turned into a "high-risk case" right in front of their eyes.

I didn't get warmth; I got a list of warnings. I didn't get a partner; I got a medical file. It made me feel like an inconvenience—just another person they had to squeeze into a packed schedule before they could go on their lunch break. It wasn’t a beginning; it was just a realization of how hard this is going to be.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest, most jarring surprise was how little I mattered in the room. I suppose I’d expected that since I’m the one carrying this baby, the doctors would be really focused on talking *to* me—asking me how I’m handling things, explaining the tests in a way that didn't feel like a lecture, or just acknowledging the difficulty of my situation.

Instead, the surprise was how quickly I became invisible. The providers would talk over me to each other, using medical shorthand, as if I weren't even sitting right there on the table. It was like I was just the vessel for the blood pressure readings. They’d look at the numbers and sigh, or shake their heads, and the tone would get so serious, but nobody ever once asked, "Jacqui, how are *you* doing today?" 

I think the biggest shock was realizing that being "high risk" actually makes you *less* of a person to them, not more. You’d think that with more medical concerns, you’d get more care or more compassion, but it felt like the opposite. The more complicated the chart, the more they distance themselves. It was like I had to earn their empathy, and I couldn't afford the price of admission. It made me feel so small, so quickly.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I thought it would be a conversation. I imagined a doctor sitting down—actually sitting, not standing by the door with their hand on the knob—and walking me through things. I expected them to explain, "This is what we saw, this is what it means for you, and here is the plan." I thought there would be a sense of being walked through it, especially since everything with the pre-eclampsia makes me so anxious. I wanted to feel like I had someone in my corner to translate all that medical jargon into something that actually makes sense for my life.

But the reality? It’s completely different. Most of the time, the results come through a portal notification on my phone while I’m on the bus or at work. It’s just numbers and medical terms popping up on a screen without any context. I’m sitting there, trying to Google what a high-risk indicator means, and I’m terrified because there’s no one to talk to. If I do get to talk to someone, it’s usually a quick, rushed phone call that leaves me with more questions than answers. They’re so focused on the data points that they forget the person on the other end of the line has to sit with those results all day, wondering if they’re doing something wrong. It’s isolating. It makes every result feel like a threat instead of information.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really hoped for a "person." You know, someone who would recognize me when I walked in, someone who wouldn't have to spend the first five minutes of every appointment asking me what my history was because they hadn’t bothered to look at my chart beforehand. I thought if I went to the same clinic, I’d see the same faces. I thought there would be a foundation of trust—that by the time I was ready to deliver, I’d be looking at a familiar face, not a stranger.

In reality? It’s a revolving door. I see a different face almost every time I’m there. One week it’s a resident who seems like they’re still learning, the next week it’s a doctor who’s already halfway out the door before I’ve finished explaining my symptoms. There is no continuity. It’s like I’m restarting the conversation every single time. 

I’m constantly having to advocate for myself, re-explaining the stressors, the transit issues, the pre-eclampsia, and the fear. It’s exhausting to have to "introduce" myself as a high-risk patient over and over again to people who clearly don't have the time to care. It’s not care, it’s just processing. They’re checking boxes on a form, and I’m just a body moving through their system, not a woman they’re actually looking after.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It makes me feel like I’m constantly shouting into a void. Practically, it’s a nightmare. I spend the first ten minutes of every appointment filling in the gaps for them because they clearly haven’t read my chart, or if they have, they’re just skimming it. I have to repeat my symptoms, my worries, and my medication list—everything—all over again. It’s like I’m doing their job for them, and when you’re already exhausted from work and the stress of this "high risk" label, that extra mental load is just brutal.

Emotionally? It makes me feel completely invisible. There is zero trust built up. When you see a different face every time, you’re never a person to them—you’re just "the patient with the pre-eclampsia" or "the pregnancy that might be difficult." You don't get that sense of someone rooting for you or someone who remembers that you were scared last time and asks if you’re doing any better. 

I don't have a safety net, so I really needed my medical team to be that constant anchor. Instead, it’s like trying to hold onto smoke. Every time I think I’ve made a connection or explained how I’m struggling, I walk into the next room and I’m a stranger again. It’s lonely, and honestly, it makes me feel like my life isn't their priority.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected them to talk to me with some basic human decency—maybe even a little bit of gentleness. You know, acknowledging that this is a huge, sometimes scary change, and asking how I’m *feeling* about it. I thought they would explain what they were doing and why, instead of just grabbing my belly or taking my blood pressure like I’m a piece of machinery that needs a quick calibration. 

But when I’m actually on the exam table? It’s all so transactional. They’re cold and brisk. There’s very little conversation; it’s mostly just "breath," "relax," or "your blood pressure is up." They examine me like I’m a science experiment, focusing strictly on the parts that are "at risk." My body doesn't feel like *mine* anymore; it feels like the hospital's property. 

It’s humiliating, honestly. I’m already feeling vulnerable because I’m alone and my body is doing things I can’t control with the pre-eclampsia, and they just amplify that. They look at my belly or my chart, but they don't look at *me*. There's no acknowledgment that I’m a human being carrying a life under a lot of stress. I leave those appointments feeling picked apart, exposed, and somehow more alone than when I walked in.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I expected that being "high risk" would mean I’d have a team—someone to help me navigate the complexity of it all. I imagined that if things like pre-eclampsia came up, there would be a nurse or a counselor who could sit down, explain the science in plain English, and maybe help me figure out how to lower my stress at work or manage the physical symptoms when I’m by myself. I thought it would be a collaborative process where my well-being—not just the blood pressure numbers—was the goal.

The reality? It’s completely isolating. There is no "team." It’s just me, alone on the T, trying to interpret cryptic test results on a portal while my head is pounding. Nobody talks to me about *how* to manage the stress; they just order more tests. When they mention the risks, it’s like they’re reading a script. They don't seem to realize that for me, the diagnosis isn't just a number—it’s a life-or-death weight I’m carrying without anyone to share the load. 

They don't tell me what to look for, how to ask my boss for accommodations, or how to calm the panic when I’m alone in my apartment at night. It’s just, "Your pressure is high, come back sooner." They treat the condition, but they completely ignore the person living with it.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything was missing. It’s like they’re trying to build a bridge across a canyon, but they forgot to build the supports. 

Information-wise, it was all technical—charts and blood pressure percentages. Nobody ever explained the *why* or the *how* in a way that made sense for my life. When I had questions about how to manage my work schedule around these appointments, they just stared at me blankly. Practical help? Forget it. Not once did anyone ask, "How are you getting here?" or "Do you have food at home?" or "Is your living situation safe?" I’m navigating public transit while my vision is sometimes blurry, and they’re acting like I have a chauffeur.

But the biggest thing was the emotional acknowledgement. I’m a human being, not a ticking medical timer. Nobody ever asked, "Are you okay doing this alone?" or "Do you need someone to talk to about being scared?" It was all, "Here’s your next appointment, don't miss it." It felt like being a prisoner of my own medical file. If they had just offered one moment of, "I know this is hard, let’s see what we can do to make it easier for *you*," it would have changed everything. Instead, I’m just a "high-risk" label, fighting for a seat on the train, wondering if I’m going to make it through the day.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a direct line—a nurse or someone I could just call and get a real answer from, someone who understood that when you’re "high risk" and alone, a weird pain or a bad headache isn't just a minor thing. I thought there would be a sense of urgency about my concerns because, you know, my health is on the line. I really believed that if I felt something was wrong, the system would be built to catch me before I fell.

But the reality? It’s a brick wall. I have to call the main clinic number, navigate a phone tree, leave a message with a service, and then... wait. I’m sitting there at my desk at work, heart hammering, watching the clock, praying someone calls back before my shift ends or before I have to leave for the bus. When they do call back, it’s rarely the same person, and they’re almost always rushing me. They treat every single question like I’m being "alarmist" or just bothering them.

It feels like there’s no room for me to be human. If I don't fit into their pre-set boxes of what constitutes an emergency, I’m basically on my own. I wanted to feel supported, but I just feel like a nuisance. I'm literally terrified that one day I’ll really need help, and I’ll just get a voicemail recording.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I guess I expected that when people heard I was pregnant—especially knowing I was going at it alone—they’d at least offer to help with the little things. I thought maybe my friends would check in, or there’d be someone to vent to when the fear got really bad. You see all these movies and commercials about "the village," and I really thought that was a real thing you just *got* when you were having a baby.

In reality? Most people don't know what to say. When I tell them I’m "high risk" and doing this solo, they get this look on their face—kind of pitying, but also uncomfortable. It’s like the "high risk" label makes people keep their distance. They don't want to hear about the blood pressure spikes or the scary, long nights on the bus. It’s too heavy for a casual conversation.

The support just... evaporated. I’m the one who has to reach out, and after a while, you stop doing that because you don't want to be the "bummer" friend who’s always stressed or scared. I don't have anyone to drop me off at the clinic, or someone to make me soup when I’m too nauseous to move. I’m completely on my own. It’s a bitter pill to realize that when things get actually hard, the "village" is mostly just a myth. I’m just navigating this silence, hoping I can keep it all together until I’m not just a "case" anymore, but a mother with a baby in her arms.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think the biggest "support" I’ve received—if you can call it that—is just people acting surprised that I’m doing this on my own. It’s like, "Wow, you’re so brave," but that doesn't actually help me pay my rent or get me to the doctor when the bus is running twenty minutes late. It feels like a pat on the back that leaves me still holding the heavy bag. 

What I really expected to materialize, and never did, was someone just stepping in to handle the logistics. I thought someone would say, "Jacqui, I'll take you to that scan on Thursday," or "Let me help you figure out how to navigate your work benefits." It’s that practical, hands-on stuff that’s missing. Instead, I get "call if you need anything," which sounds nice but is basically useless when you don't even know where to start or who to ask.

It’s strange—I’ve had total strangers on the subway give me their seat, which is kind, I guess. But the people who actually know me, the ones I thought would be in my corner? They’ve mostly just drifted away. It’s like my life got too complicated for their idea of a "fun" friendship, or they just don’t know how to handle someone who’s constantly, legitimately struggling. I’m learning to be my own village, which is just another way of saying I’m completely exhausted.

It’s just... the weight of it all. People don't tell you that being "high risk" is a job. It’s an unpaid, 24/7, high-stakes project manager role that I never applied for. 

I’m standing at the stop for the 1 bus, watching the digital sign say "Delayed," and I know that every minute I’m standing here, I’m stressing, which makes my heart race, which makes my blood pressure spike, which gives the doctors more "data" to lecture me about at the next appointment. It’s a vicious cycle that no one at the clinic seems to acknowledge. They want me to "lower my stress," but they don't give me the tools to do it. They don't look at my life—my commute, my bank account, my empty apartment—and say, "Okay, let's fix the *environment* so the body can follow." They just want the symptom to disappear so the chart looks nice.

Sometimes I think the only thing getting me through is this stubborn anger. I’m angry that I’m being treated like a statistic. I’m angry that the "miracle of life" is being turned into a series of bureaucratic hurdles that seem designed to keep me exhausted. I just want someone to look at me—not my blood pressure, not my insurance, not my "high risk" label—and see a woman who is trying to survive. I’m just trying to get through today so I can get to tomorrow, and I really hope that for this baby, it’s going to be enough. Is that all there is? Just holding on until I hit the finish line?
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

God, yes. All the time. It’s like the second I walk into that exam room, my voice loses its power. I’ve had doctors tell me to "just stop working" or "cut back on hours" like it’s a choice I’m making for fun. I’m sitting there, sweating over how I’m going to pay for my prenatal vitamins, and they’re lecturing me about my stress levels as if I can just go home and take a nap. They don't hear me when I say, "If I don't work, I don't eat." It’s like they’re living in a different universe where everyone has a savings account to rely on.

And it’s not just the doctors. My family—those who even bother to check in—will give me these "well-meaning" pieces of advice that just make me feel like I’m failing. "You should be doing this," or "You really need to be eating more of that." They don't see the reality of my kitchen or the transit map or the sheer exhaustion that comes from being alone in this. 

It feels like my body, my life, and my decisions are all up for debate, but nobody is actually listening to the person living inside them. They all have these opinions on how I should manage this "high-risk" pregnancy, but none of them are standing at the bus stop at 8:00 PM with their heart pounding. They have the luxury of being "right," and I have the burden of living with the consequences of their advice—or their lack of it.

It’s like I’m living in two different worlds. One world is what the doctors and the apps talk about—that "magical" gestation where everything is predictable and easy. And then there’s my world, where every single day is a negotiation with my own body and a city that doesn't care if I’m tired or if I’m scared.

I’m sitting here now, just looking at my phone, wondering if I should call the clinic about this headache that won’t go away. Then I think, *if I call, they’ll tell me to come in.* And if I come in, I’ll have to miss the last two hours of my shift, which means I lose that pay, which means I’m short on groceries for the weekend. So I sit here. I drink some water. I try to breathe. I become my own gatekeeper for my own care because the system makes it so impossible to access help without paying a price I can’t afford.

It’s just... it shouldn't be this hard to just exist, let alone grow a baby. I just want to get to the point where I can see him, hold him, and finally stop being "high risk." I just want to stop being a "case." I want to be a mom who doesn't have to fight for her life and her baby's life on the same platform where everyone else is just going home from work. It’s so much, you know? It’s just so much.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

Since I'm doing this alone, I haven't had a partner's voice in my ear telling me what to do—which, honestly, is a double-edged sword. On one hand, I don’t have someone pressuring me to align with *their* vision of how this should go. But on the other hand, the pressure isn't coming from a person; it’s coming from the system itself, and that’s almost worse.

When I talk about my life, my hours at work, or the realities of how I get around, the clinic acts like those are "my choices." They’ll say, "Well, you really need to prioritize rest," as if I have a shelf I can just pull "rest" off of. It’s a subtle kind of pressure—like they’re judging me for not being the "ideal" patient who can just stop their life to prioritize the pregnancy. It’s a different kind of pressure than a partner, but it feels just as demanding. They expect me to conform to a lifestyle I can’t afford, and when I can't, I’m the one who ends up feeling like the problem. 

I’m the only one here to make these decisions, and every time I have to choose between my baby’s health and the paycheck that keeps us housed, I feel like I’m failing someone. I didn't have a partner to push against, but I’ve got this giant, cold medical machine pushing me into a corner every single time I walk through those doors.

You know, the thing that’s really getting to me lately is how little choice I feel like I actually have. Everyone acts like you’re "making a choice" to take a job or a certain shift, but when your back is against the wall, those aren't choices—they’re survival strategies. 

When a doctor tells me, "You really need to cut back your hours if you want your blood pressure to stay down," they aren't offering a solution; they’re just stating a problem that I already know exists. It’s like being told you’re drowning and being advised to "swim more gracefully." It’s condescending, even if they don't mean it to be. 

And then there’s this constant, low-level guilt. If I’m not doing the "right" thing for the baby, I’m the bad mother, even though I’m working myself to the bone *for* the baby. I just feel like I’m constantly being measured against a standard that was built for people who have help, who have cars, who have time, and who don't have to worry about the rent on the first of the month. 

I’m exhausted by being the one who has to explain my poverty to people who don't want to hear it. I’m tired of feeling like I’m failing because I can’t live up to the "ideal" pregnancy. Sometimes I just want to walk into that clinic and be treated like a human being who is doing the impossible, not a case file with a list of "non-compliant" behaviors. I’m just trying to make it to the end of this, and honestly? I’m starting to wonder if I’ll ever feel like "myself" again, or if this permanent state of being "high risk" is just who I am now.

I catch myself staring at the other women in the waiting room sometimes. You know the type—they’ve got the comfy yoga pants, someone is usually with them checking their phone, they look like they’ve actually slept. I find myself getting this… I don't know, this sharp, hot resentment. Not even at them, really, but at the system that makes their "high risk" look so different from mine. For them, "high risk" is a medical condition to be managed with rest and extra care. For me, "high risk" is an extra shift I have to pick up to cover the specialist copays, or the panic of the Red Line stalling while I’m worried about my BP.

It’s like I’m running a race in lead boots while everyone else is in sneakers, and the doctors are standing on the sidelines telling me, "You really should be running faster to get to the finish line." 

It’s exhausting to have to constantly explain why I can’t "just take it easy." I want to be able to just be a mother, you know? Not a patient. I want to be a person who gets to enjoy the idea of this baby, not someone who’s constantly bracing for the next bad scan result or the next bill that I have no idea how I’m going to pay. I feel like I’m losing pieces of myself every day to this fear and this poverty. I just want to survive this so I can finally, finally, just be someone's mom. That’s all I’m holding onto. Just that one thing.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Honestly, I mostly turn to my phone. But not the apps—I mean the anonymous forums or threads where other women are actually being real. I’ll search for things like "working while pregnant with high BP" or "how to manage anxiety during a solo pregnancy," just to see if anyone else feels like the system is failing them. It’s not exactly a "community," and it’s definitely not a support system, but it’s the only place where I don't feel like I’m crazy for being scared.

Sometimes I text a girl I worked with a few years back. She’s the only person I know who had a "complicated" pregnancy. I don’t even tell her everything because I don't want her to worry or look at me with that same pitying, uncomfortable expression everyone else has. But just getting a text back saying, "I know, it sucks, just breathe," feels like a lifeline. It’s pathetic, right? That a one-sentence text is the closest I get to actual support?

But that’s where I go first. Before I call the doctor, before I even tell my mom, I check the internet or send that one quick text. Because I already know what the doctor is going to say—they’re going to tell me what I’m doing wrong. When I talk to these other people, even if they’re strangers on the internet, they don't judge me. They just get it. It’s a very small, quiet relief, but it’s all I’ve got.

The thing is, I think I’m afraid of being a "bad patient." When you’re high-risk and low-income, you feel like you’re walking on glass. If I’m honest with my provider about how I’m really feeling—about the panic, the fear of losing the baby, or even just how hard it is to pay the transit fare—I’m terrified they’ll start looking at me as a "complicated" person who needs to be fixed or, worse, someone who isn't "fit" for this.

So I filter everything. I go into that office, I put on my "calm face," I answer the questions with the "right" answers, and I leave feeling like a total stranger to my own pregnancy. It’s like I’m performing a play called "Jacqui is Doing Fine" so that I don't get labeled as a problem. 

But behind the curtain? The fear is just eating me alive. I think about the birth, and my throat literally tightens up. I imagine myself in that hospital room, all alone, and I start to wonder if I’m strong enough to handle whatever comes next. It’s this weird, secret loneliness. I wish, just once, someone would look past the chart and say, "I know you're doing this by yourself, and I know it’s scary. Tell me what you need." But they don't. And I’m too tired to ask. I just keep showing up, keep paying the fare, and keep praying that we both make it to the other side.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I can count the times my input felt "sought" on one hand, and honestly, even those felt like a test I was supposed to pass. Most of the time, it’s just me being told what’s happening next: "You need this scan," "You need this blood panel," "Your medication is changing." It’s always a statement, never a question. They’re not consulting me; they’re issuing instructions, and my only job is to figure out how to make it happen within my budget and my schedule.

There was one nurse, though—just one—who actually paused for a second during a blood pressure check. She looked at me, really looked at me, and asked, "How is the bus ride today? Is it getting easier?" It was such a small thing, but it caught me so off-guard that I almost started crying right there on the table. It wasn't a medical question; it was human. For those few seconds, it felt like she recognized that I was a person having a rough day, not just a set of vitals.

But that’s the exception, not the rule. The rest of the time, I’m just a box they’re ticking. The "plan" is already decided before I even walk into the room. If I try to speak up or mention that a certain time doesn't work for my job, I can feel the mood in the room sour. It’s like, *why are you making this difficult?* So, I just stay quiet. I nod, I take the appointment card, and I leave. It’s not a partnership; it’s a lecture I’m paying to attend.

It’s just... the more I talk about it, the more I realize how much of myself I’ve had to cut off just to fit into their world. I’ve become this quiet, polite, compliant patient because that’s the only way to avoid being labeled as "difficult." Being labeled "difficult" is a luxury I don't have. If I’m difficult, maybe they stop seeing me. Maybe they stop trying. I don't know. The fear that they’ll just give up on me because I’m "too much" of a case to manage is always in the back of my mind.

But the toll? It’s heavy. When you spend all your time pretending everything is fine just to keep the doctors happy, you start to lose your grip on what you actually need. I’m so focused on checking the boxes for the clinic that I don't even know how to ask for help with the stuff that matters—like the fact that I’m dreading the birth because I have no one to hold my hand, or that I’m so exhausted I can barely stand on the train. 

I’m just drifting. I’m doing exactly what they ask, I’m showing up to every single appointment, and I’m paying every single fare. And at the end of the day, I’m still just one person in a dark apartment, worrying about whether I’m going to be enough for this baby. I’m waiting for someone to notice that there’s a real person behind the blood pressure monitor, but I’m starting to think that’s just a dream I’m having. It’s hard to keep being "brave" when you feel like you’re just waiting for the next thing to go wrong.

It’s funny, I talk about being "brave," but that’s just a word people use to make me feel better about being trapped. When you're in my position, you don't have the luxury of being "brave"—you just have the necessity of surviving. 

I think about the future, about the birth, and I honestly don't know how I'm going to get through those hospital doors. I picture that room, the sterile lights, the nurses who don't know my name, and the silence where a partner should be. It’s not just the pain I’m afraid of; it’s the absolute, crushing isolation of it all. I’m scared that if I cry out, there won't be anyone there to really *hear* it. I’m scared that they’ll see my "high-risk" chart and just jump to the most clinical, invasive interventions because they don’t have the time or the patience to wait on me. 

I’m exhausted, you know? I’ve spent months now being the perfect patient, showing up on time, paying the fares, staying quiet, and keeping my blood pressure as low as I can manage under the weight of it all. I just want this to be over. I want to hold my son and finally, finally be allowed to stop "managing" everything. I’m tired of being a project. I’m tired of being a risk. I’m just a woman, and I’m so tired of being alone in this.

You know, what’s really starting to hit me is how much I’ve stopped believing that anyone is actually looking out for me. I keep coming back here—to this clinic, to these appointments, to these sterile rooms—but the more I do it, the more I realize I’m just walking into a system that’s been designed to filter people like me out, not pull us in. 

Every time I go, I find myself looking at the doctors and the nurses and thinking, *do you know what I had to do just to get here today?* Do you know I missed a shift? Do you know I haven't eaten a real lunch in two days because I had to save that money for the CharlieCard? And then they look at my numbers, and they make a face, and they tell me to "relax." It feels like they’re speaking a different language. 

I’m so lonely. It’s not just the absence of a partner; it’s the absence of anyone saying, "Jacqui, you’re doing something incredibly hard, and I’m proud of you for making it this far." Instead, I get "your levels are off" and "we’ll see you next week." I’ve spent my whole pregnancy holding my breath, waiting for someone to finally see *me*—not the pre-eclampsia, not the insurance paperwork, not the "high-risk" label. But I’m starting to think that’s not going to happen. I’m just a woman on a bus, heading toward a delivery I’m terrified of, hoping that when the time comes, I’ll find the strength to do what I have to do. Because honestly? I don't think anyone else is going to do it for me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, I’ve been sitting here thinking about what I’d tell them—the people who run these clinics or the ones who design these apps. It’s not about adding more appointments or more monitors. I’ve got enough of those to last a lifetime.

It’s about the silence. That deafening, heavy silence when I’m sitting in that office and nobody asks, "How are you *actually* doing today?" 

I think what’s most important to me is that I want them to know that I’m not just a set of blood pressure readings or a "high-risk case" to be solved. I’m a woman who had to choose between a meal and a transit pass just to get to that waiting room. I’m a woman who sits on the bus and stares at her own reflection, terrified that if I let one tear fall, I might not be able to stop it. I’m doing the work of two people—the patient and the caregiver—and nobody ever acknowledges that.

If there’s one thing I wish I could change, it’s that. I wish they could see me. I wish they could understand that every single appointment isn't just a medical check-in; it’s a struggle I had to overcome just to be present. I just want to be seen as human, not as a chart. That’s all. I’m just a woman, and I’m just trying to make it to the other side.
